perturbation.original_text: 6.1 The Company will pay the Executive a salary of £740,000 and a fixed benefits allowance of £30,000 per annum. The salary (and so far, as is reasonably possible) the benefits allowance will accrue from day to day and be payable in equal instalments in arrears on or around the 25th day of every month, less deductions for income tax and National Insurance contributions and shall be inclusive of any fees receivable by the Executive as a director of any Group Company.
perturbation.changed_text: 6.1 The Company will pay the Executive a salary of £740,000 and a fixed benefits allowance of £30,000 per annum. The salary (and so far, as is reasonably possible) the benefits allowance will accrue from day to day and be payable in equal instalments in arrears on or around the 15th day of every month, less deductions for income tax and National Insurance contributions and shall be inclusive of any fees receivable by the Executive as a director of any Group Company.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces a contradiction in the payment date. Initially, the salary is paid 'on or around the 25th day of every month.' The modified text changes this to 'on or around the 15th day of every month.' If there is a reference to the 'standard payment date' elsewhere in the contract, this contradiction creates ambiguity. For example, if section 15 references that the payment of any outstanding salary shall be paid to the executive on the standard payment date. This would be an in-text contradiction and it would create uncertainity on whether the standard payment date is the 15th of the 25th.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 15.2 The Company may in its sole and absolute discretion (whether or not any notice of termination has been given under sub clause 15.1) terminate this Agreement at any time and with immediate effect by giving notice in writing to the Executive that the Company is exercising its rights pursuant to this clause 15. If the Company elects to terminate the Executive's employment in this way, it will make, within 30 days, either the first instalment (of equal monthly instalments) of a, or an entire, payment in lieu of notice (Payment in Lieu) equal to the basic salary, benefit allowance and any benefits, as at the Termination Date, which the Executive would have been entitled to receive under this Agreement during the notice period referred to at sub clause 15.1 (or, if notice has already been given, during the remainder of the notice period), less all relevant deductions for income tax and National Insurance contributions. For the avoidance of doubt, the Payment in Lieu shall not include any element in relation to: (a) any bonus or discretionary payment(s) that might otherwise have been due during the period for which the Payment in Lieu is made; and (b) any payment in respect of any holiday entitlement that would have accrued during the period for which the Payment in Lieu is made.
perturbation.justification: The modified text changes the payment date from the 25th to the 15th of the month. Section 15.2 discusses payments upon termination which are determined using the 'basic salary, benefit allowance and any benefits, as at the Termination Date'. If the termination date falls at a time when the standard payment date is supposed to be the 15th, but an earlier version stated the 25th, this causes a contradiction and uncertainty as to the payment date, thus satisfying the definition of 'Ambiguities – In Text Contradiction'.

perturbation.original_text: 11.1 The Company's holiday year runs from 1 January to 31 December (the Holiday Year). The Executive is entitled to 25 days' paid holiday in addition to the usual public or bank holidays in England) in every Holiday Year, to be taken at times convenient to the Company.
perturbation.changed_text: 11.1 The Company's holiday year runs from 1 January to 31 December (the Holiday Year). The Executive is entitled to 20 days' paid holiday in addition to the usual public or bank holidays in England) in every Holiday Year, to be taken at times convenient to the Company.
perturbation.explanation: This perturbation reduces the number of paid holidays to 20 days, creating an in-text contradiction if other sections of the contract assume 25 days. For example, if section 11.3, which discusses payment in lieu of holiday, assumes a calculation based on 25 days. If 11.3 is calculating payments in lieu of holidays based on the premise of the executive is entiteld to 25 days. it will contradict, as the executive is only entitled to 20 days.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 11.3 The Company reserves the right to require the Executive to take any outstanding holiday during any period of notice of termination of employment or to make a payment in lieu of holiday outstanding at the Termination Date. If, at the Termination Date, the Executive has taken more holiday than he has accrued, the Executive hereby expressly consents to the Company deducting an appropriate amount from any payments otherwise due him. Deductions and payments in lieu of holiday are to be calculated on the basis that a day's holiday is equal to 1/260 of the Executive's basic salary.
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the entitled paid holiday from 25 days to 20 days. The contradicted text, section 11.3, calculates deductions and payments in lieu of holiday based on a 'day's holiday is equal to 1/260 of the Executive's basic salary,' which assumes 260 working days including the original 25 days of holiday, thus creating a contradiction. The change to 20 days invalidates the 1/260 calculation. This is an 'Ambiguities – In Text Contradiction' as the calculation of the payments is based on a premise (25 days), that the Changed Text modifies to 20 days, and thus causes a contradiction. This satisfies the definition, by altering the Executive's entitlement.

perturbation.original_text: 15.1 The Appointment may be terminated by either party giving the other at least 12 months' notice in writing.
perturbation.changed_text: 15.1 The Appointment may be terminated by either party giving the other at least 6 months' notice in writing.
perturbation.explanation: This changes the minimum notice period for termination to 6 months from 12. This creates a conflict with subsequent references to the original 12-month notice period, which occurs in clause 15.2. The payment in lieu of notice in clause 15.2 is calculated on the 12 months. This will create a contradiction if the executive is only required to give a 6 months notice period.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 15.2 The Company may in its sole and absolute discretion (whether or not any notice of termination has been given under sub clause 15.1) terminate this Agreement at any time and with immediate effect by giving notice in writing to the Executive that the Company is exercising its rights pursuant to this clause 15. If the Company elects to terminate the Executive's employment in this way, it will make, within 30 days, either the first instalment (of equal monthly instalments) of a, or an entire, payment in lieu of notice (Payment in Lieu) equal to the basic salary, benefit allowance and any benefits, as at the Termination Date, which the Executive would have been entitled to receive under this Agreement during the notice period referred to at sub clause 15.1 (or, if notice has already been given, during the remainder of the notice period), less all relevant deductions for income tax and National Insurance contributions. For the avoidance of doubt, the Payment in Lieu shall not include any element in relation to: (a) any bonus or discretionary payment(s) that might otherwise have been due during the period for which the Payment in Lieu is made; and (b) any payment in respect of any holiday entitlement that would have accrued during the period for which the Payment in Lieu is made.
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the minimum notice period from 12 months to 6 months (clause 15.1). Clause 15.2 refers to the notice period in 15.1 when calculating the payment in lieu of notice, which is calculated based on the 12-month period. Therefore, the change creates a contradiction because the payment in lieu would still be calculated on 12 months even though only 6 months' notice is required.
